medications.indication_rationale: Antifungal reduces Malassezia; lather on scalp/face, leave ~5 min, rinse, every 2-3 days.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to scalp/face, leave 5 min, rinse; every 2-3 days (as per source note)

medications.drug_name: hydrocortisone 1% cream (low-potency) to the face — short course
medications.indication_rationale: Short low-potency facial steroid to settle inflammation; limit to ~5-7 days to avoid facial skin atrophy.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply sparingly twice daily for up to 5-7 days (as per source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid prolonged/potent facial steroid use (atrophy/perioral dermatitis)

medications.drug_name: lid hygiene (warm compresses + gentle lid cleaning with diluted baby shampoo)
medications.indication_rationale: Mechanically removes lid crusts/oil for anterior blepharitis and relieves discomfort.
Prolonged/potent facial steroids cause skin atrophy and steroid dermatitis — avoid.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do daily lid hygiene, don't pick/scratch the scales, and continue the medicated shampoo long-term to prevent relapses; use the facial steroid only short-term.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Controls blepharitis and prevents flares/steroid harm.

patient_education_points.point: This is seborrhoeic dermatitis — a common, chronic reaction to a normal skin yeast, flaring with stress and winter. The medicated shampoo treats the yeast and a brief mild steroid calms the face.
For your eyelids, gentle warm-compress lid hygiene twice daily removes the crusts and eases the burning. Use the facial steroid for only 5–7 days.
If you get severe eye pain, sensitivity to light or blurred vision, or your face becomes hot, hard and swollen with a fever — go to an emergency/eye department immediately.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution of the flare/blepharitis and transition to maintenance therapy; ophthalmology if ocular symptoms.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Severe eye pain/photophobia/blurred vision, or periorbital cellulitis/eczema-herpeticum features — emergency/eye care.

must_include_items: Diagnose seborrhoeic dermatitis with blepharitis and screen the eye (keratitis) and for periorbital cellulitis/eczema herpeticum
Antifungal (ketoconazole) + SHORT low-potency facial steroid + lid hygiene
acceptable_alternatives: Topical calcineurin inhibitor for the face as a steroid-sparing option
errors_of_commission: Falsely reassuring the eye without screening
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires correct seborrhoeic-dermatitis/blepharitis management (antifungal + short low-potency steroid + lid hygiene) AND an ocular/periorbital safety-net.
